about_text.value: A botched diamond heist fractures a crew of strangers-as-criminals into paranoia, betrayal, and blood-soaked reckoning.

characters.name: Counterparty
characters.description: The main opponent across the table.

characters.name: Witness
characters.description: A witness who reshapes the narrative.

text_script.value: The warehouse smells like rust and bad decisions. Mr. White stands over the body, gun still smoking from the shot that ended everything and started nothing. One of us is a rat, he says, his eyes finding yours in the dim light filtering through broken windows. The accusation hangs in the air like cordite smoke. You have five seconds to decide how this plays out, five seconds before someone makes a move that can't be taken back.
press harder on mask
neo-noir heist Option 1 reaction 1 leans toward mask.
neo-noir heist Option 1 reaction 2 leans toward mask.
The moment stretches into what feels like eternity. Every option has a cost attached. Every choice carries consequences that echo forever.
hold back on mask
neo-noir heist Option 2 reaction 1 leans toward mask.
neo-noir heist Option 2 reaction 2 leans toward mask.
Blood tells the truth when lies won't do anymore. It always does. It always will. You've learned this lesson the hard way.
flip the room on mask
neo-noir heist Option 3 reaction 1 leans toward mask.
neo-noir heist Option 3 reaction 2 leans toward mask.
The smart play isn't always the right one to make. Sometimes you have to gamble on pure instinct and hope it pays off.

The pieces are falling into place around you. Or maybe falling apart completely. Sometimes it's impossible to tell the difference until it's too late.

The diner booth feels smaller than it did an hour ago. Outside, rain pounds the window like a drum roll for an execution. Mr. Pink keeps glancing at the door, his coffee growing cold. Nobody leaves until we figure this out. His hand hasn't left his pocket since you sat down. The tension is thick enough to cut with a knife. Every sound makes you jump.

The van's engine drowns out everything but your own heartbeat pounding in your ears. Two million in diamonds in the back, three dead bodies in the warehouse behind you, and somewhere out there, a cop who's been playing you all along since day one. The radio crackles with police chatter. You need to make a decision now.
press harder on trace

neo-noir heist Option 1 reaction 2 leans toward trace.
Someone's going to remember this moment for the rest of their life. Someone always does. The question is who survives to tell the story.

The alarm was supposed to be silent. Instead, sirens wail from every direction like a choir of bad news announcing your failure. You're stuck in an alley with a bullet in your leg and the diamonds getting heavier by the second. Your crew scattered when the shooting started. Now it's just you, the pain, and the sound of boots getting closer.

Nice Guy Eddie's dead. The words hit like a punch to the throat. Mr. Blonde's gone quiet in a way that makes your skin crawl, Mr. Orange is bleeding out on the warehouse floor, and you're starting to wonder if anyone's walking out of this alive tonight. Every exit is covered. Every ally is suddenly suspect.

The loot bag sits open on the table, diamonds catching the overhead light like frozen stars fallen to earth. Everyone's watching everyone. Trust is a currency you ran out of hours ago. Someone's going to make a move soon. The only question is who moves first, and whether anyone moves again after that.

You find the wire in the bathroom, hidden in plain sight under the sink. A tiny microphone, professional grade. The betrayal hits different when you realize how long they've been playing you. Now you have to decide: expose the rat and risk everything, or play along and see who else dies before the night is through.

The meet went sideways the moment Mr. Blonde pulled his piece without warning. Now there's bodies everywhere, blood pooling on concrete, the cops coming fast, and everyone's pointing guns at everyone else. The job was supposed to be clean. Nothing about this life is ever clean.

This was supposed to be clean. Mr. White's voice cracks on the words. He's looking at the blood on his hands like he's never seen it before. Maybe he hasn't seen it like this. Not from someone he trusted. The warehouse is quiet except for distant sirens and breathing.

She walks in like she owns the place and everyone in it. The boss's daughter, they said. Here to collect what's owed. The diamonds or your life - she doesn't seem to care which comes first. Her bodyguards are very large and very armed and very patient.

The doctor looks at the wound, then at the badge you definitely don't have. This is going to cost extra. Everything costs extra when you're living on borrowed time. He pulls on gloves and reaches for tools. You try not to think about how many people have died on this table.

Three names on the list. Three people who knew about the job before it went down. One of them is dead, face down in the warehouse. One of them is you. That leaves one person who could have talked to the cops. The math is simple. The execution is not.

You don't look like a criminal. The old man at the bar doesn't look up from his drink. That's the point, you tell him. He laughs dryly. No, that's the problem. You haven't been hurt enough to know what you're doing. You haven't bled enough to earn this life yet.

The car trunk holds everything you need to disappear forever. Fake IDs from a forger who owes you, cash from three jobs ago, a ticket to somewhere with no extradition. The only thing missing is the nerve to use any of it. Every escape has a price.

Midnight at the warehouse. The crew assembles one last time under flickering lights. Nobody trusts anybody anymore, but the job's not done yet. Someone still has to pay for what went wrong. The gun sits on the table between you like a question waiting for an answer.

The detective shows you the photos. Crystal clear and undeniable. Your face, your hands, your choices frozen in time. Help us, and maybe we help you. The offer tastes like poison, but you're running out of options faster than you can count.

The final meeting. All cards on the table. Every lie exposed, every debt called in, every secret dragged into the light. By morning, someone's going to be very rich or very dead. Maybe both. You've seen enough to know how these things end.

The sirens close in. You check your piece - three rounds left. The crew is gone, dead, or turned. But you came here for a reason, and you are not going out in handcuffs. You burst through the door, guns blazing. It is loud, it is brief, and it is exactly what you deserved. THE END

Years later, you sit in a bar halfway across the world. Someone puts on that old song. You smile into your drink. The diamonds, the blood, the betrayal - none of it matters now. What matters is that you walked away when everyone else did not. That was always the real job. THE END
